coredata.dc:title: A facile and controllable protocol for simultaneous synthesis of magnetite nanoparticles and luminescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Kui
Wang, Lanhui
Tian, Fei
Du, Kunze
Chang, Yanxu
Han, Lifeng
Yao, Pei
coredata.dc:description: Both magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) and luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were simultaneously synthesized via a hydrothermal route at temperature as low as 200 °C. The synthesized magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs were readily separated and purified from the reaction mixture, without the need for additional energy input and chemicals. The magnetic properties of purified Fe3O4 and photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of purified CDs were investigated using Squid-VSM magnetic property measurement system and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The size of Fe3O4 NPs and the luminescent properties of CDs were tunable by changing the reaction time or ratio between starting materials within a certain range. Fe3O4 NPs exhibited high saturation magnetization in the range of 36.59–68.22 emu/g and their magnetic behavior significantly depend on the size and structure of Fe3O4 NPs. This work was expected to provide a kind of green and high efficiency simultaneously synthesis of size-controllable Fe3O4 NPs and photoluminescence tunable CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simultaneous synthesis
Magnetite nanoparticles
Luminescent carbon dots
Superparamagnetic
Hydrothermal process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the resultant mixture of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
Separation and purification process of the Fe3O4 NPs and CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We want to express our thanks to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81102802 and 81774148 ) and the China Scholarship Council Fund for financial support (CSC No. 201408120006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low magnification TEM image of the Fe3O4 NPs (b) High-resolution TEM image of the Fe3O4 NPs (c) Selected-area-electron-diffraction pattern of the Fe3O4 NPs (d) XRD pattern.
Magnetization curve of Fe3O4 NPs (≈160 nm) obtained at 300 K, normalized to the mass of Fe3O4.
(a) Typical TEM image of luminescent CDs, inset is HRTEM of one carbon dot (b) the corresponding size distributions (c) Raman spectrum and (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
PL spectra of CDs excited by a different wavelength.
UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectrum of sample S1.

PLE and PL spectra of three CDs solution prepared by adding 0.025 g, 0.05 g and 0.01 g NaBH4(named as S1, S2, and S3), the PL spectra were collected excited at their corresponding PLE peak 440 nm, 410 nm and 400 nm, respectively.
Schematic illustration for the simultaneous synthesis of magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs via hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 12 h.

head.ce:title: A facile and controllable protocol for simultaneous synthesis of magnetite nanoparticles and luminescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Instrumentation Centre, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of TCM Chemistry and Analysis
Tianjin University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kui Lin and Lanhui Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) and luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were simultaneously synthesized via a hydrothermal route at temperature as low as 200 °C. The synthesized magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs were readily separated and purified from the reaction mixture, without the need for additional energy input and chemicals. The magnetic properties of purified Fe3O4 and photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of purified CDs were investigated using Squid-VSM magnetic property measurement system and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The size of Fe3O4 NPs and the luminescent properties of CDs were tunable by changing the reaction time or ratio between starting materials within a certain range. Fe3O4 NPs exhibited high saturation magnetization in the range of 36.59–68.22 emu/g and their magnetic behavior significantly depend on the size and structure of Fe3O4 NPs. This work was expected to provide a kind of green and high efficiency simultaneously synthesis of size-controllable Fe3O4 NPs and photoluminescence tunable CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) have been thought to be one of the most attractive nanomaterials and have extensive applications in clinical diagnosis and treatment [1], biological labeling and tracking [2], drug delivery [3], medical separation and purification [4] due to their unique physical and chemical properties. Many efforts have been made to the synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs through many kinds of methods. Much of these efforts have generated tremendous growth in the emerging of various strategies and methods for the synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs with different size and morphology [5,6]. Currently, as well recognized and widely used methods for their convenience and reliable are co-precipitation technique, thermal decomposition reaction, and hydrothermal reaction [7].

Li et al. suggest that the monodisperse Fe3O4 microspheres with controllable size and composition can be synthesized through the solvothermal reduction method, this method involves the ferric (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium acetate (NaAc), and ethylene glycol (EG) [8]. Moreover, the fact in this approach should arouse our attention that EG molecules were generally used to serve as precursors for the formation of CDs through carbonization process [9–12].

Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) with unique structure and specific surface functional groups have also been widely explored for their great perspective in a wide range of applications such as a multimodal bio or chemical sensing [13], bioimaging and biolabeling [14,15], optoelectronic devices [16–18], catalyst supports [19,20], and so on. For example, due to their strong and tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, CDs were suggested as promising surface functionalization hosts for bioimaging and chemosensing. The optical properties of CDs have been proven to be tunable depend on their preparation methods and precursors as described in the literature [21–23]. Up to date, various functionalized CDs have been successfully synthesized via different protocols by employing chemicals, bulk carbon materials, and natural plants as starting materials [23]. Particularly, the synthesis of CDs using chemicals via hydrothermal or solvothermal carbonization of molecular precursors, such as EG Ref. [9], citric acids [24,25], and chitosan [26,27], is considered as the most powerful approach for the tunable synthesis of CDs with desired PL properties. Among a variety of synthesis protocols for CDs, the facile and efficient hydrothermal method using EG and appropriate tuning chemicals was intensive investigated compared with other methods [10,28,29]. For those protocols, the formation of CDs has been ascribed to the carbonization of EG due to the occurring of intermolecular and/or intramolecular dehydration reaction with the help of appropriate dehydrating agent [9]. Moreover, EG is a strong reducing agent at high temperature so can be used to restore the metal ions from high valence to low valence to synthesize metal or metal oxide nanoparticles [8,30].

In the present work, Fe3O4 NPs and CDs were simultaneously synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of a homogeneous mixed solution containing iron (III) nitrate enneahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and ethylene glycol (EG) at 200 °C. The as-prepared product was a mixture of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs, but the product was then separated easily by magnetic separation and purified in the laboratory, which reduces environmental issues owing to the ease of operation and high productivity.

The role of each chemical in the synthesis route was explored and determined with several comparison experiments. The emphasis of this work was placed on the combination of two typical procedures for simultaneous synthesis of intensively investigated nanomaterials: magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs, which was believed to have a few of characteristics of green chemistry, including maximize the transformation of raw material into final products, low energy input, use of safe and environmentally benign substances, low emission of waste.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), iron (III) nitrate enneahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), ethylene glycol (EG) Iron (III) and chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) is from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Deionized water is used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the resultant mixture of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A representative sample was prepared by modifying a typical procedure for the synthesis of Fe3O4 microspheres [8]. Ethylene glycol (EG) was selected as solvent for iron (III) nitrate enneahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4). Briefly, 1.062 g Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.025 g NaBH4 was dissolved into 40 mL EG to form a yellow solution and a clear solution, respectively. Then the above two solutions were mixed together to form brownish red solution under ultrasonication for 10 min. The resulting 80 mL solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h, then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. It was found that the color of the starting solution changed from brownish red to dark gray; the as-prepared homogeneous and stable solution emitted bright blue under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S1). Interestingly, the as-prepared mixture of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs showed stable and good-dispersibility before separation and purification treatment, and did not aggregate or precipitated for more than 1 month as placed in the open air, which was important for the further treatment and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation and purification process of the Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared product was thoroughly separated and purified through a simple and energy saving approach as follows: Firstly, the as-prepared product was poured into a vial without centrifugation. Secondly, the black Fe3O4 NPs were firmly attracted together due to their magnetism in the presence of an external magnet outside the vial wall (Fig. S2). Thirdly, the supernatant containing CDs was poured into another vial for the further treatment. Finally, the Fe3O4 product was rinsed and purified thoroughly with deionized water for several times. The CDs solutions were purified against deionized water using dialysis tubing for 24 h. Thus, the purified Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs solution were obtained. It should be emphasized that the conversion of the iron element from Fe(NO3)3·9H2O into Fe3O4 could be as high as 98%. Several comparative samples were prepared by varying the reaction time and concentration of the precursors as shown in Table S1.

The phase structure of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs was investigated using a Rigaku D/max 2500v/pc XRD. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 with a field emission gun to examine the morphology and structure of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs. The composition was analyzed using energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) purchased from EDXA, INC. The magnetic properties of the Fe3O4 NPs were recorded with a Squid-VSM Magnetic Measuring System (Squid-VSM). All PL and PLE spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary EclipseVarian) and UV–Vis absorption was performed on a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Cary). Chemical and functional groups were characterized with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer operating at room temperature in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy was recorded using a Renishaw inVia Reflex confocal Raman microscope with
λ
exc
= 532
nm and the power was set to 45 mW for CDs and 1 mW for Fe3O4.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, uniform spherical Fe3O4 NPs with very narrow size distribution were observed after purification using TEM. The average size of Fe3O4 NPs was around 160 nm (estimated from more than 100 particles). Fig. 1b showed the representative high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of one Fe3O4 NP. The inset in Fig. 1b showed that one Fe3O4 NP consisted of a great number of quantum-sized particles with an average size 7 nm, which was well accorded with single crystal size determined by the XRD result and revealed their polycrystalline properties. The interplanar distance was measured to be 0.29 nm, which corresponds to the (220) spacing of Fe3O4. The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 1c implied the polycrystalline features of as-prepared Fe3O4 NP, the diffraction ring was ascribed to the {111},{220},{311},{222},{440},{422} plane of the cubic Fe3O4. In addition, the quantitative EDS result shown in Fig. S3 revealed that the Fe3O4 NPs were only composed of Fe and O. It should be noted that three copper peaks originate from the copper grid. The crystalline structure of Fe3O4 NPs was characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD pattern can be easily assigned to Fe3O4 (JCPDS File No.75-1609). The average single crystal size of Fe3O4 NPs is about 8 nm calculated by the Scherrer equation, which was very close to the sizes estimated from HRTEM. Three broad bands were seen in Raman spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs as shown in Fig. S4. The Raman bands at 354 cm−1 and 500 cm−1 were assigned to Eg and T2g mode, respectively. Eg represents the symmetric and asymmetric bends of oxygen with respect to Fe, while T2g represents asymmetric stretch of Fe and O. Most importantly, the Raman bands at 698 cm−1 was designated as A1g mode, which refers to the symmetric stretch of oxygen atoms along FeO bonds [31].

The above results indicated that obtained products adsorbed and separated by an external strong magnet were pure Fe3O4 NPs with an average size of 160 nm. The uniform spherical Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized using the modified procedure and recipe via facile and green hydrothermal process.

Fe3O4 NPs is an important magnetic nanomaterial. The magnetic measurements were performed using SQUID-VSM. The magnetization curves measured at 300 K were shown in Fig. 2 . It is very clearly seen that no coercivity and remanence existed, indicating the superparamagnetic property of as-obtained Fe3O4 NP products. The magnetic saturation values are 57.43 emu/g, which is smaller than that of bulk material (92 emu/g) and single-crystalline Fe3O4 with diameters of 200 nm (81.9 emu/g) [8].

TEM image and size distributions of CDs in Fig. 3 indicated that all CDs were dispersed uniformly on TEM grid with an average size of 4 nm. The HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 3a revealed the high crystallinity of the CDs and the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.34 nm, which was very close to that of (111) planes of the graphite. The size histogram in Fig. 3b implies size of the CDs were in the range of 3–5 nm. The Raman spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3c displayed a weak G band at around 1620 cm−1, indicating the existence of carbon with good crystallinity. The Raman results agrees well with the HRTEM analysis. Moreover, Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to determine the surface groups of the CDs. Broad absorption bands at 3200-3400 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 3d was attributed to OH and NH stretching vibration. Absorption bands at 1560 cm−1, 1385 cm−1 were assigned to NH bending vibrations, CH bending vibrations, respectively. Significantly, OH and NH groups was very common in hydrophilic CDs.

The typical CDs presented excitation-dependent PL behaviors as shown in Fig. 4 , which was usually observed in luminescent CDs synthesized from EG. The PL spectra of the CDs was akin to that of CDs prepared using EG and NaBH4 in the previous report [9,28,29], while redshifts about 40 nm in the peak PL emission.

Fig. 5 presents the UV–vis absorption and PL spectrum of S1. The UV–vis spectrum show a weak absorption shoulder around 260 nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds. The CDs solution shows a strong PL emission peak centered at 530 nm when excited at 440 nm.

The radiative recombination of surface-confined electron and holes were widely recognized emission mechanism for CDs. The introduction of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen or chloride, into the surface of CDs, was supposed to create a new surface electronic state, leading to the tuning of optical properties [32]. The optical properties of synthesized CDs was really affected by the addition amount of NaBH4 into the reaction system within a limited range. More NaBH4 resulted in a slightly blue shift of CDs’ strongest PL peak, while the size of Fe3O4 NPs did not increase with the amount of NaBH4 added to the reaction system. Fig. 6 shows PLE and PL spectra of 3 CDs prepared by adding a different amount of NaBH4 into the reaction system. The strongest PL peaks of 3 CDs prepared by adding 0.025 g, 0.05 g and 0.01 g NaBH4 were around 530 nm, 500 nm, and 490 nm, respectively. The corresponding PLE peaks of 3 CDs were around 440 nm, 410 nm, and 400 nm, also exhibiting a blue shift. The PLE and PL shifting of CDs into blue region might be the results of increasing boron-doping concentration [33].

The experimental results also suggested that precursor concentration did have a remarkable effect on the morphology of synthesized Fe3O4 NPs under given reaction time, which was different from the result as reported in the previous works [8]. For example, when the concentration of Fe3+ was reduced by 50% (Sample S4), the fluffy Fe3O4 NPs with a uniform diameter of 160 nm were observed using TEM (Fig. S5). Doubling the concentration of Fe3+ (Sample S5) in the reaction system actually resulted in the Fe3O4 NPs intermixed with foam-like graphitic carbon (Fig. S6), the products obtained and the results are in agreement with those reported by He et al. [34]. As we all know that nitrates have a strong oxidizing capacity, especially at high temperature [35,36]. The formation of foam-like graphitic carbon could be attributed to strong oxidation of the excessive nitrate radical in the starting solution at the temperature of 200 °C.

At the same precursor concentration and reaction temperature, the average diameter of Fe3O4 NPs was subsequently increasing with the reaction time. Controlling reaction conditions for precursor concentration, the diameters of Fe3O4 NPs were around 100 nm at a reaction time of 6 h, 160 nm at 12 h, and 200 nm at 16 h (Fig. S7). More importantly, Fe3O4 NPs with a diameter 100 nm displayed looser structure and lower crystallinity compared to other 2 samples. Their corresponding magnetic saturation curves also displayed superparamagnetic property, and magnetic saturation values were determined to be 36.59 emu/g, 57.43 emu/g, and 68.22 emu/g, respectively (Fig. S8).

According to the Néel-Arrhenius equation, with the particle size decreasing, the magnetic saturation also decreases. Furthermore, crystallinity also influences the magnetic properties of nanoparticles. Accordingly, the magnetic saturation of Fe3O4 NPs is smaller that of the corresponding bulk materials and decreases with the decrease of the particle sizes. However, the reaction time and the concentration of Fe3+ did not influence the PLE and PL properties of CDs in all controlling experiments.

The above results indicated that the diameters and crystallinity of Fe3O4 NPs increased with increasing reaction time, but the size of CDs did not. However, it is hard to derive a quantitative correlation between particle diameter and changes in reaction time or precursor concentration due to the complex physical and chemical processes during the formation of Fe3O4 NPs.

The simultaneous synthesis of the Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The adding of NaBH4 into Fe(NO3)3·9H2O solution resulted in the brownish red solution, which is attributed to the formation of iron at the lower valence state. After hydrothermal reaction at the 200 °C for 12 h, the color of the reaction mixture changed from brownish red to dark gray. The as-prepared products was then separated to obtain the pale yellow supernatant and magnetic Fe3O4 NPs precipitate in the presence of an external magnets. It was found that the supernatant emitted bright blue under UV irradiation at 365 nm, while the morphology of precipitate was spherical NPs as examined by the TEM.

Owing to its reducibility and high boiling point, ethylene glycol (EG) is widely used to prepare fine metal or metal oxide nanoparticles under the hydrothermal condition. In this reaction system, NaBH4 played important roles both in the formation of Fe3O4 NPs and shifting the PL peak of CDs in this reaction system. On the one hand, NaBH4 acted as both reducing agent and electrostatic stabilizer. After mixing of NaBH4 with Fe3+ solution, iron in a free state at the lower valence was firstly formed due to the strong reducibility of NaBH4. Then the quantum-sized Fe3O4 were formed and gradually aggregates into large Fe3O4 NPs in the presence of reductive EG under hydrothermal conditions for a quite long time. On the other hand, NaBH4 is also believed to not only facilitate the dehydrogenation reactions between OH groups [9] in EG for the formation of CDs but also tune photoluminescence properties via boron-doping [32] into CDs during the hydrothermal process. According to above results, the superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NP with controllable size and CDs with tunable photoluminescence in a limited range could be simultaneously synthesized by simply varying the reaction time, the concentration of starting precursors. Both EG and NaBH4 served as reducing agent together to reduce the Fe3+ for the formation of Fe3O4 NP, the carbonization of EG under the assistance of NaBH4 result in the formation of luminescent CDs with tunable peak emission.

In summary, simultaneously synthesis of superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs with a controllable diameter in the range of 100–200 nm and luminescent CDs with tunable PL peak emission in the range of 490–530 nm has been realized by adding proper precursors by this one-step, simple, and green hydrothermal process. Well-dispersed superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs were separated and purified easily from the as-synthesized mixture in the presence of an external magnetic field.

The control experimental results showed that the reaction time, the concentration of precursors was the key factors for the synthesis of such two fundamentally different nanomaterials. The simultaneous formation mechanism of magnetic Fe3O4 NPs and luminescent CDs was briefly described. Magnetic measurements displayed that Fe3O4 NPs possess polycrystalline and ferromagnetic properties, the size and crystallinity of Fe3O4 NPs have an influence on their magnetic properties. This work provides one new perspective for the simultaneously and green synthesis of currently popular nanostructures, which will minimize the use of chemicals and generation of chemical waste.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We want to express our thanks to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81102802 and 81774148) and the China Scholarship Council Fund for financial support (CSC No. 201408120006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.08.021.
